title: Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements
subtitle: Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.
caption:
Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements

Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.

The chart displays the cumulative number of parties for twelve distinct multilateral environmental agreements from 1971 to 2015. The Vienna Convention (Ozone) and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) show the earliest and most extensive adoption, with both lines rising from near zero in the early 1970s to converge near the 200-party mark by 2015. The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and the UN Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD) follow similar trajectories but begin their ascent later, around 1992 and 1994 respectively, reaching approximately 190 and 180 parties by the end of the period. A middle cluster of agreements, including the World Heritage Convention, the Kyoto Protocol, and the Cites Convention, shows varied start dates between the 1970s and 1990s, with final values ranging between 150 and 175 parties. The Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants and the Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety exhibit steep growth starting in the late 1990s and early 2000s, reaching roughly 170 and 160 parties respectively. The remaining agreements, such as the Ramsar Convention, the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), the Rotterdam Convention, and the Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals, display more gradual or delayed increases, with final counts falling between 120 and 160 parties. Most lines demonstrate a sigmoidal growth pattern, characterized by a period of rapid increase followed by a plateau as the number of parties stabilizes.
